planting.note: Set transplants out once nights stay reliably above 50F after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep indoors about six weeks before your last frost and grow on under bright light. Cage or stake this vigorous indeterminate vine and water evenly to prevent blossom-end rot. Pick the elongated, thick-walled paste fruit when fully deep red for the richest sauce.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors once frost danger has fully passed.

directions: Sow shallowly indoors and grow strong seedlings before transplanting into rich, well-drained soil. This Polish heirloom produces long, banana-shaped red paste fruit on a rangy indeterminate plant that benefits from sturdy staking. Pick when the fruit is fully colored and the flesh feels dense.

planting.note: Set out transplants after the last spring frost.

directions: Begin seed indoors about six weeks before your frost-free date and harden off gradually. This indeterminate paste type bears red fruit streaked with wavy orange-gold stripes and holds well on the vine. Harvest when the base color deepens to red and the striping is pronounced.

planting.note: Plant out after frost when soil temperatures reach the mid-sixties.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant seedlings deep so the buried stem roots out. Long, curved, pepper-shaped red fruit forms in clusters on a tall indeterminate vine that needs support. Pick when fully red for a flavorful, low-seed paste tomato.

planting.note: Transplant outdoors once the danger of frost is past.

directions: Start seed indoors under lights and set out well-hardened plants after frost. This indeterminate heirloom produces very long, tapering red paste fruit in generous clusters. Harvest when the elongated tomatoes are evenly red and firm.

planting.note: Set plants out after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow seed shallowly indoors about six weeks before transplanting. This indeterminate paste variety bears large, sausage-shaped red fruit with thick, meaty walls. Pick when fully red for sauces and canning.

planting.note: Move seedlings to the garden once frost has passed.

directions: Start indoors and transplant deeply into fertile ground after the weather settles. This indeterminate heirloom yields long red paste fruit with dense flesh well suited to cooking. Harvest when the tomatoes color up fully and feel solid.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a sunny, warm bed.

directions: Sow indoors six weeks before setting out and harden off before planting. This compact determinate bush produces a heavy set of small, pear-shaped red paste fruit that resists cracking. Harvest the whole cluster once the tomatoes are uniformly red.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost date.

directions: Start seed indoors under lights and transplant strong seedlings into rich soil. This indeterminate vine bears long, tapered red paste fruit averaging several ounces each. Pick when fully red for concentrated, low-moisture sauce.

planting.note: Transplant to the garden after the last frost.

directions: Begin seed indoors and set hardened plants out after frost danger passes. This Italian paste heirloom bears long, slender red fruit in dense finger-like clusters on a semi-determinate plant. Pick clusters when the fruit is fully red for drying or sauce.

planting.note: Set out after frost when nights are warm.

directions: Sow indoors six to eight weeks ahead and transplant deeply into rich soil. This Italian oxheart bears large, heart-shaped red fruit with dense, low-seed flesh ideal for slicing and sauce. Support the heavy fruit and harvest when fully colored and just yielding to the touch.

planting.note: Transplant into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors and grow on to sturdy transplants before setting out. This indeterminate variety produces long, banana-shaped red paste fruit with thick walls and rich flavor. Harvest when the tomatoes are fully red and heavy for their size.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a hot, sunny location.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant into well-drained, fertile soil after frost. This old Italian variety bears deeply ribbed, flattened red fruit favored for cooking down into sauce. Harvest when fully red; the pronounced ridges are typical of the variety.

planting.note: Transplant after the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Start indoors and set out well-hardened seedlings into warm soil. This heirloom paste type produces long red plum fruit with meaty interiors and good yield. Pick when the fruit colors fully for canning or sauce.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm garden soil.

directions: Start seed indoors and set out hardened plants after the last frost. This tall indeterminate heirloom produces abundant small red pear-shaped fruit over a long season. Harvest frequently once the fruit is fully red for snacking and preserves.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors after the last frost.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deeply after frost into fertile soil. This German heirloom, whose name means giant bunch of grapes, bears huge trusses of small red fruit with a pointed tip. Harvest the clusters as the fruit ripens to full red.

planting.note: Transplant to the garden after frost.

directions: Sow indoors and set out sturdy seedlings after the weather warms. This indeterminate variety bears long trusses of round, port-wine colored cherry fruit with a sweet flavor. Harvest as the fruit deepens to a reddish-brown hue.

planting.note: Set out after frost; give this sprawler plenty of room.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant this wild currant-type into a large cage or trellis. It produces enormous quantities of tiny, intensely sweet red fruit on a rampant, disease-tough vine. Harvest the small tomatoes continuously as they ripen to deep red.

planting.note: Transplant after frost and allow room to sprawl.

directions: Sow indoors and set out this wild-species tomato into a roomy support. It bears sprays of pea-sized bright red fruit with a bright, tangy flavor. Strip the delicate fruit from the sprays as it colors up.

planting.note: Set out after frost with generous spacing.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into a large cage after frost. This currant-type bears clusters of tiny, pale creamy-yellow fruit with a mild sweet taste. Harvest the small fruit when it turns from green to a soft ivory color.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; a good pick for short seasons.

directions: Sow indoors and set out this compact determinate after frost. It ripens early, bearing round golden-yellow cherry fruit that is nearly seedless and sweet. Harvest the whole cluster as the fruit reaches a rich gold.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant a hardened plant after frost. This semi-determinate variety bears clusters of round fruit that ripen to a soft amber-green with sweet, zingy flavor. Pick when the fruit softens slightly and shows a yellow-green blush.

planting.note: Transplant to the garden after frost.

directions: Sow indoors and set out a strong seedling after frost danger passes. This indeterminate vine produces bicolored gold-and-red cherry fruit, often with a marbled star at the blossom end. Harvest when the fruit shows its full red-blushed gold color.

planting.note: Set out after frost into warm soil.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant after frost. This German heirloom, whose name means little blonde girl, bears very large trusses of small golden-yellow cherry fruit that resist cracking. Harvest the trusses as the fruit ripens to a clear yellow.

planting.note: Transplant after frost with room to climb.

directions: Sow indoors and set out a sturdy plant after frost. This vigorous indeterminate vine yields abundant small red cherry fruit with a bright, sweet-tart flavor. Pick often once the fruit ripens to full red to keep it coming.

planting.note: Set out transplants after frost.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant after the last frost. This robust indeterminate heirloom produces heavy crops of round, cherry-sized red fruit even in heat and humidity. Harvest continuously as the fruit ripens to deep red.

planting.note: Transplant to the garden after the last frost.

directions: Sow indoors and set out a well-hardened plant after frost. This popular indeterminate heirloom bears long trusses of round red cherry fruit with a sweet, well-balanced flavor. Pick regularly as the fruit ripens fully red.

planting.note: Set out after frost into warm soil.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant after the danger of frost passes. This curiosity produces knobby, clustered red fruit that looks like many small tomatoes fused together and can be pulled apart in pieces. Harvest when the whole knobby fruit is fully red.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; give it a tall support.

directions: Sow indoors and set out this vigorous vine after frost. It bears masses of tiny red fruit, little bigger than a pea, packed with concentrated tomato flavor. Harvest the small fruit continuously as it turns deep red.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant after frost into fertile soil. This indeterminate vine produces round, pale ivory-yellow cherry fruit with a mild, sweet, fruity flavor. Pick when the fruit ripens from green to a creamy pale yellow.

planting.note: Transplant to the garden once frost is past.

directions: Sow indoors and set out a sturdy plant after frost. This Siberian heirloom bears round golden-yellow cherry fruit with a sweet flavor and tolerates cool conditions well. Harvest when the fruit ripens to a clear yellow.

planting.note: Set out after frost with a tall support.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant a hardened plant after frost. This indeterminate vine bears heavy trusses of round, pale ivory-yellow cherry fruit with a mild, sweet flavor. Pick regularly as the fruit reaches a soft creamy yellow.

planting.note: Transplant after frost and allow room to sprawl.

directions: Sow indoors and set out this wild currant-type after frost into a roomy cage. It produces masses of tiny pale-yellow fruit with a sweet, fruity flavor on a rampant vine. Harvest the small fruit as it ripens to soft ivory-yellow.

planting.note: Set out after frost into warm soil.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant after frost. This indeterminate variety bears enormous branched trusses of small, yellow pear-shaped grape fruit. Harvest the loaded clusters as the fruit ripens to bright yellow.

planting.note: Transplant to the garden after the last frost.

directions: Sow indoors and set out a strong seedling after frost. This indeterminate heirloom bears large red cherry fruit with a rich, sweet flavor on long trusses. Pick the fruit as it ripens to a full glossy red.

planting.note: Set out after frost into a warm bed.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant after frost. This Iowa heirloom produces small, fuzzy-skinned pale-yellow fruit with a mild, sweet flavor, borne in clusters. Harvest when the fruit blushes creamy yellow and feels slightly soft.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow indoors and set out a hardened plant after frost. This Russian heirloom produces small, oval mahogany-brown plum fruit with a rich, sweet flavor, good for fresh use or drying. Harvest when the fruit deepens to reddish-brown.

planting.note: Set out transplants after frost into a sunny bed.

directions: Sow seed indoors about six weeks before transplanting and grow strong seedlings. This determinate paste variety produces a concentrated set of egg-shaped red fruit with thick walls and few seeds. Harvest the crop as the fruit ripens to a solid deep red for sauce and canning.

planting.note: Transplant after frost with a tall cage.

directions: Start seed indoors and set out a sturdy plant after frost. This indeterminate vine bears clusters of small, oblong red grape-shaped fruit with firm, sweet flesh. Pick often as the fruit ripens to deep red.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost.

directions: Sow indoors and set out after frost into fertile soil. This heirloom is prized for fruit that stores for weeks after picking, ripening slowly indoors into winter. Harvest the pale orange-red fruit before frost and keep it in a cool, dry place.

planting.note: Set out after frost into warm garden soil.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant after frost. This heirloom paste type bears long, curved, sausage-shaped red fruit with meaty, low-seed flesh on a productive semi-determinate plant. Harvest when the elongated fruit is fully red.

planting.note: A cool-tolerant Siberian type that still needs frost-free nights to set well.

directions: Sow shallow in warm flats and transplant once the danger of frost has passed. Stake the productive vine and feed lightly to keep the round garnet fruit coming. Pick when the skin deepens to a smoky red-brown and the flesh feels slightly soft.

planting.note: A large black beefsteak that rewards a full warm season and steady feeding.

directions: Raise transplants under strong light and plant them deep once soil is warm. Provide a sturdy cage and keep the root zone evenly moist to reduce shoulder cracking. Pick when the big fruit turns dusky purple-brown with darker olive shoulders and softens slightly.

planting.note: A compact potato-leaf black variety suited to short seasons and containers.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant after frost into rich, well-drained soil. This shorter determinate-leaning vine still benefits from a cage and even watering. Pick when the round fruit flushes reddish-brown with green shoulders and internal veining shows through.

planting.note: Produces heavy clusters of smooth, medium black-red fruit through the season.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and set them out deep once frost has passed. Support the tall vine well since it sets fruit in clusters of six or more. Harvest when the smooth globes turn deep dusky brown-red and feel just tender.

planting.note: A high-anthocyanin variety whose skin turns near-black where sunlight strikes it.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant after frost into a spot with strong direct sun to develop the darkest color. Cage the vine and water evenly for smooth, plum-sized fruit. Harvest when the underside turns from green to reddish and the fruit yields softly, since the black top ripens first.

planting.note: A brown-skinned selection from Cherokee Purple with the same rich flavor.

directions: Start transplants indoors six weeks early and plant deep after frost. Cage the indeterminate vine and mulch to hold even moisture under the heavy beefsteaks. Harvest when the skin turns a uniform mahogany-brown and the fruit softens slightly at the base.

planting.note: An old deeply ribbed purple heirloom with intense, tart flavor.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deep after the last frost. Support the vine and water steadily, accepting that the fruit is heavily pleated and irregular. Pick when the ruffled fruit turns dusky purple-red and gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: A large Oaxacan bicolor beefsteak with orange and pink-red marbling.

directions: Start seed indoors and plant hardened transplants deep after frost. Support the vigorous vine well and mulch for steady soil moisture. Cut when the fruit glows orange with red streaks and feels heavy for its size.

planting.note: A gold-and-red bicolor beefsteak that colors up richly late in the season.

directions: Raise transplants indoors and set them deep once frost danger passes. Cage the tall vine and keep moisture even to limit shoulder cracking. Harvest when the golden fruit develops a ruby blush and yields gently.

planting.note: A mild low-acid bicolor beefsteak, yellow-gold blushed with red.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and set them out deep after frost. Support the tall vine and water evenly to keep the very large fruit from splitting. Pick when the golden fruit shows red mottling and feels heavy and slightly soft.

planting.note: A large Southern bicolor heirloom with gold flesh streaked red.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deep after frost into warm, rich soil. Cage the vigorous vine and mulch for even moisture under the heavy fruit. Harvest when the gold fruit shows red streaking and yields slightly at the base.

planting.note: A striped beefsteak with alternating gold and red stripes over the skin.

directions: Start transplants indoors and plant them deep once frost has passed. Cage the vine and keep watering steady to keep the striped skin from cracking. Harvest when the gold-and-red stripes brighten and the fruit softens slightly.

planting.note: An early British variety bearing small red fruit striped with gold.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant after frost, giving the vine a stake to climb. This heavy cropper sets early, so pick often to keep new fruit coming. Harvest when the red fruit shows clear gold stripes and gives slightly.

planting.note: A striped beefsteak with dark pink fruit and green metallic stripes.

directions: Raise transplants indoors and plant them deep after frost. Cage the vine and keep moisture even to reduce cracking on the flattened fruit. Harvest when the fruit deepens to port-wine pink with olive-green striping and softens slightly.

planting.note: A large dark beefsteak, mahogany-red overlaid with green-bronze stripes.

directions: Start transplants indoors and set them deep after frost. Cage the vigorous vine and mulch for steady moisture under the heavy fruit. Slice when the fruit turns brick-brown with olive striping and yields to gentle pressure.

planting.note: A small dusky-red salad tomato striped with green, rich and sweet.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and transplant after frost, giving the vine a trellis. Pick regularly to keep the clusters productive through the season. Harvest when the fruit turns deep reddish-brown with green stripes and softens slightly.

planting.note: A green-when-ripe striped beefsteak with amber and green marbling.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deep after frost into full sun. Cage the vine and water evenly, judging ripeness by feel since color stays green. Harvest when the fruit takes on an amber-green blush and gives to a gentle squeeze.

planting.note: A sweet green-when-ripe cherry that turns amber-gold at maturity.

directions: Start indoors and transplant after frost, giving the tall vine strong support. Pick frequently since the sweet clusters set heavily all season. Harvest when the green cherries turn slightly golden-amber and feel soft.

planting.note: A dwarf-habit black tomato from the Dwarf Tomato Project, ideal for pots.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant after frost into a large container or bed. The sturdy rugose-leaf plant stays compact but still benefits from a light stake. Harvest the dusky-purple fruit when it colors to smoky rose and softens slightly.

planting.note: A dwarf pink beefsteak from the Dwarf Tomato Project suited to containers.

directions: Start indoors and transplant after frost into rich potting mix or garden soil. The compact plant carries heavy fruit, so give it a short stake and even water. Pick the dusky-pink fruit when it fills out and yields to gentle pressure.

planting.note: A compact dwarf bearing brick-brown fruit on a short, bushy plant.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant after frost into a container or small-space bed. This short bushy plant needs little staking but appreciates steady water. Harvest the medium fruit when it turns dusky mahogany-red and softens slightly.

planting.note: A dwarf-habit variety producing large dark red-brown beefsteaks on a short plant.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant after frost into deep, rich soil. The thick-stemmed dwarf holds heavy fruit but benefits from a short stake in wind. Pick when the large fruit turns deep dusky red and gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: A dwarf green-when-ripe beefsteak from the Dwarf Tomato Project.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant after frost into a large pot or bed. The compact plant carries big green fruit, so support it lightly and water evenly. Harvest when the green fruit takes on an amber-green blush and softens under a gentle squeeze.

planting.note: A dwarf yellow beefsteak from the Dwarf Tomato Project with mild sweet flesh.

directions: Start indoors and transplant after frost into a roomy container or bed. The rugose-leaf dwarf stays short but sets sizable fruit, so give it a light stake. Pick when the pale-yellow fruit fills out and yields gently.

planting.note: A dwarf green-when-ripe variety with sweet lime-green flesh.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant after frost into a container or compact bed. This short bushy dwarf needs minimal support but appreciates even moisture. Harvest when the green fruit turns faintly amber-green and gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: A dwarf pale-ivory beefsteak from the Dwarf Tomato Project with mild flavor.

directions: Start indoors and transplant after frost into a large pot or bed. The compact plant holds large fruit, so add a short stake and water steadily. Pick the creamy-white fruit when it softens slightly and loses its green undertone.

planting.note: An old dwarf variety producing surprisingly large pink fruit on a short plant.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant after frost into a container or small bed. The stout dwarf carries heavy fruit for its size, so provide a light stake. Harvest the pink fruit when it fills out fully and gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: A historic 1880s dwarf-habit pink tomato with sturdy potato-leaf growth.

directions: Start indoors and transplant after frost into a container or compact bed. This thick-stemmed old dwarf stands largely upright with only a light stake. Pick the pink fruit when it colors fully and yields to a gentle squeeze.

planting.note: A tiny dwarf cherry perfect for windowsills, pots, and short seasons.

directions: Sow indoors and grow in a small pot on a sunny sill or transplant outdoors after frost. The miniature plant needs no staking but must not dry out in its container. Harvest the small red cherries when fully colored and just soft, picking often.

planting.note: A very compact dwarf cherry bred for pots and windowsill growing.

directions: Start seed indoors and keep the plant in a small container in strong light. This miniature dwarf needs no support but benefits from steady watering. Pick the small red cherries as they ripen fully and soften.

planting.note: One of the smallest tomatoes, a true micro-dwarf for tiny pots and sills.

directions: Sow indoors and grow in a small pot in bright light, indoors or on a patio. The tiny plant needs no staking but dries out fast, so water lightly and often. Harvest the small red fruit when fully colored and just soft.

planting.note: One of the darkest tomatoes, with high anthocyanin turning the skin near-black.

directions: Start indoors and transplant deep after frost into a spot with full sun to maximize the dark pigment. Cage the vine and water evenly for large, meaty fruit. Harvest when the shoulders turn inky black-purple and the underside flushes red-brown and softens.

planting.note: A Russian black beefsteak with flattened, deeply colored fruit.

directions: Raise transplants indoors and set them deep after frost. Support the vine and mulch to keep moisture steady under the large flattened fruit. Harvest when the fruit turns deep purplish-brown with darker shoulders and gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: A hollow stuffing tomato, red with gold stripes and a firm bell-pepper shape.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deep after frost into warm soil. Stake the vine and water evenly to keep the hollow fruit sizing up. Harvest when the ribbed fruit turns red with gold striping and feels firm-ripe.

planting.note: Start seed indoors well before the last frost and set out only once nights stay warm.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm trays and transplant sturdy seedlings after the soil has warmed. Keep plants evenly moist and stake the slender stems as the long fruit sets. Pick pods when they turn glossy red for the hottest flavor, or leave a few to dry on the plant for grinding.

planting.note: Transplant after all danger of frost has passed and the ground is thoroughly warm.

directions: Start seed indoors in warm conditions and harden off before setting plants into rich, well-drained soil. Feed lightly and water steadily to avoid blossom drop during hot spells. Harvest firm green pods for classic flavor, or let them ripen red and develop corky lines for extra sweetness and heat.

planting.note: Set transplants out once the soil is reliably warm in late spring.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and transplant into full sun after nights warm up. These tall plants bear heavily, so mulch to conserve moisture and support laden branches. Pick the small cylindrical pods green for bright heat, or allow them to color red on the plant.

planting.note: Give this slow starter an early indoor sowing and a long warm season.

directions: Sow seed early indoors with bottom heat, since germination is slow and uneven. Transplant only after the weather is settled and warm, then feed regularly through the long ripening period. Harvest the lantern-shaped pods once they turn full orange for fruity, intense heat.

planting.note: Start indoors early and wait for warm nights before transplanting.

directions: Germinate seed in warm, moist trays and grow on until nights stay above the fifties. Set plants in full sun with steady feeding and even watering to keep fruit setting. Pick the wrinkled pods when they glow bright orange for the fullest aroma.

planting.note: A long-season type best started very early indoors.

directions: Sow with consistent warmth, as these seeds are slow to sprout. Grow seedlings strong before transplanting into a sunny, sheltered bed with rich soil. Wait for the pods to deepen to a dark brown color, which signals full ripeness and smoky heat.

planting.note: Start early indoors for this hot, long-season habanero.

directions: Begin seed indoors with heat and transplant when the season is fully warm. Provide a long, hot growing period and mulch to hold soil moisture. Harvest the crinkled pods once they ripen bright red for searing heat and citrus notes.

planting.note: A Caribbean favorite needing early sowing and steady warmth.

directions: Sow seed early with warmth and patience, then grow seedlings until the weather settles. Transplant into a warm, sunny spot and feed through the season for heavy fruiting. Pick the squat, bonnet-shaped pods when fully colored for their signature fruity heat.

planting.note: An Indian superhot that needs a long, hot growing season.

directions: Start seed early with consistent warmth and be patient through slow germination. Set robust plants out only when nights are warm, then support the tall, productive stems. Harvest the rough, tapered pods once they ripen deep red, wearing gloves to handle their fierce heat.

planting.note: A record-setting superhot requiring the longest, warmest season available.

directions: Germinate seed early indoors with steady heat and grow strong transplants for a long season. Plant into warm, fertile soil and feed regularly to sustain heavy fruiting. Pick the lumpy red pods when fully colored, and always wear gloves given the extreme capsaicin levels.

planting.note: A famed superhot best given an extra-early indoor start.

directions: Sow seed early with warmth and grow seedlings on until settled warm weather arrives. Transplant into a sunny bed and support the productive plants as pods form. Harvest the scorpion-tailed red pods when ripe, taking care to protect skin and eyes from the intense oils.

planting.note: A dark-podded Trinidadian superhot needing a long hot season.

directions: Start seed early indoors with strong warmth for reliable sprouting. Grow sturdy plants and transplant into full sun once frost danger is well past. Harvest the pebbly pods when they ripen to a deep chocolate brown, using gloves for these extremely hot fruits.

planting.note: A South Asian superhot closely related to the ghost pepper.

directions: Germinate seed early with heat and grow seedlings on for a long season. Transplant into warm, rich soil in full sun and feed steadily as fruit sets. Pick the wrinkled red pods when fully ripe, handling them with gloves for their blistering heat.

planting.note: An African chinense prized for citrusy heat and long harvest.

directions: Sow seed early indoors with warmth and grow on until nights turn mild. Transplant into full sun with fertile soil and consistent watering. Harvest the slender, wrinkled pods when they turn bright yellow for their lemony aroma and fierce bite.

planting.note: A tiny wild Peruvian pepper with a bushy, ornamental habit.

directions: Germinate seed early with warmth, as this wild type can be slow to sprout. Grow the bushy plants in full sun or a large container and feed lightly. Harvest the pea-sized golden pods as they ripen for a fruity, aromatic heat prized in Peruvian cooking.

planting.note: A compact, heavy-bearing pepper ideal for pots and small beds.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant compact seedlings once the soil warms. These bushy plants set dozens of upright pods and do well in containers. Harvest the slim pods green or let them ripen red, drying easily on the plant for later use.

planting.note: A small, fiery Southeast Asian pepper on a tidy bush.

directions: Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant after frost into full sun. The plants bear masses of tiny pointed pods and thrive in containers. Pick the small pods when red for maximum heat, or use them green in fresh dishes.

planting.note: The classic sauce pepper, upright-podded and heat-loving.

directions: Sow seed early indoors with warmth and grow strong plants for transplanting. Set into full sun and a long warm season, feeding to sustain the tall, bushy growth. Harvest the small upright pods when they turn bright red, the classic stage for hot sauce.

planting.note: A Brazilian frutescens staple with small, upright pods.

directions: Germinate seed indoors with warmth and grow on for a long season. Transplant into full sun and mulch to retain moisture through hot weather. Harvest the small pointed pods when they ripen red for their sharp, lasting heat.

planting.note: The small African chile behind piri piri sauce.

directions: Start seed early indoors with warmth and transplant after frost into full sun. The bushy plants bear many small pointed pods over a long season. Pick the pods when bright red for the sharp, aromatic heat used in African and Portuguese cooking.

planting.note: A mild long green chile for roasting and stuffing.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant into warm, well-drained soil after frost. Keep evenly watered so the long pods size up smoothly. Harvest the fruit green for roasting, or let it ripen red for a sweeter, slightly hotter chile.

planting.note: An early, medium-hot wax pepper good for short seasons.

directions: Sow seed indoors and set transplants out once the soil has warmed. These early plants tolerate cooler regions and bear well. Harvest the smooth pods yellow for mild heat, or wait as they ripen through orange to red for more punch.

planting.note: A jalapeno-like pepper often used ripe and red.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant into full sun after the last frost. Water evenly and mulch to keep fruit setting through summer heat. Harvest the conical pods green for milder flavor, or let them ripen red for a sweeter, fruitier heat.

planting.note: A conical wax-type pepper with mild to medium heat.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant when the soil is warm and settled. The compact plants bear heavily and take container culture well. Pick the pods pale yellow for mild flavor, or leave them to ripen orange and red for added heat.

planting.note: A round Mexican chile that rattles when dried.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant into a warm, sunny bed after frost. Grow through a long season and water steadily for full pod development. Harvest the small round fruit when deep red, then dry them for the nutty flavor prized in Mexican sauces.

planting.note: A slender Mexican chile that dries to a bright red stick.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant into full sun after the soil warms. The tall plants bear many narrow pods over a long season. Harvest the slim fruit when red and dry them whole for grinding into a sharp, clean heat.

planting.note: A small, wild-type Mexican chile with intense heat.

directions: Germinate seed early with warmth, as this near-wild type is slow to sprout. Transplant into full sun and lean soil, watering sparingly once established. Harvest the tiny oval pods when bright red for their fierce, quick heat.

planting.note: A Peruvian aji with citrusy flavor and cheerful yellow pods.

directions: Sow seed early indoors and grow on through spring for a long season. Transplant into full sun and support the tall, productive plants. Harvest the crinkled pods when bright yellow for their tangy, lemony heat.

planting.note: A distinctive three-cornered baccatum pepper.

directions: Germinate seed early indoors and grow strong plants for the long season. Transplant into full sun and give the large plants room to spread. Harvest the flying-saucer-shaped pods when red for sweet flesh with heat concentrated near the seeds.

planting.note: A citrus-scented South American aji on a tall plant.

directions: Start seed early indoors and grow seedlings on until the weather warms. Transplant into full sun and stake the tall stems as pods develop. Pick the slim yellow pods once ripe for their bright, lemony heat.

planting.note: An Andean pepper with black seeds that tolerates cooler nights.

directions: Sow seed early indoors with warmth and grow on for a long season, as this species is slow. Transplant into full sun with rich soil, and note it handles cool nights better than most peppers. Harvest the thick-walled, apple-shaped pods when fully red or orange, noting their unusual black seeds.

planting.note: The classic long green roasting chile of the Southwest.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant into warm, well-drained soil in full sun. Water steadily and feed to size up the long, straight pods. Harvest green for roasting, or let the fruit ripen and dry red for ristras and ground chile.

planting.note: A New Mexico State chile known for very long pods.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant into full sun once the soil has warmed. Keep well watered so the very long pods develop evenly. Harvest green for roasting and stuffing, or allow the fruit to ripen red for a sweeter, hotter chile.

planting.note: A Mexican chile that dries into guajillo.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant into full sun after the last frost. Grow through a long, warm season and water steadily as the pods form upright. Harvest the tapered fruit when deep red, then dry it to make guajillo chiles.

planting.note: A long, dark Mexican chile dried for mole sauces.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant into warm, rich soil in full sun. Water evenly through the long season so the slender pods develop fully. Harvest the fruit when dark brown-red, then dry it into pasilla chiles for sauces and moles.

planting.note: A widely used dried Mexican chile with mild, berry-like heat.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant into full sun after frost has passed. Keep evenly moist through the season to size up the long, tapered pods. Harvest the fruit deep red and dry it for the tangy, mild chile used across Mexican cooking.

planting.note: A carrot-orange heirloom chile on a compact plant.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant into full sun once the soil warms. The tidy plants set colorful pods well and suit containers. Harvest the tapered fruit when bright orange for a crisp, fruity heat.

planting.note: A very hot chinense with wrinkled yellow pods.

directions: Germinate seed early with warmth and grow strong seedlings for the long season. Transplant into full sun and feed to sustain heavy fruiting. Harvest the wrinkled pods when bright yellow for their fruity aroma and fierce heat.

planting.note: A Trinidadian superhot said to spice seven pots of stew.

directions: Sow seed early indoors with strong warmth for dependable germination. Grow robust plants and transplant into full sun once the season is warm. Harvest the pebbly red pods when fully ripe, wearing gloves to handle the extreme heat.

planting.note: A blistering Trinidadian chile with a distinct pointed tail.

directions: Start seed early with steady warmth and grow strong plants for a long season. Transplant into full sun and feed regularly through heavy fruiting. Harvest the tailed red pods when ripe, protecting hands and eyes from the intense oils.

planting.note: A mild French Basque chile traditionally dried and ground.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant into full sun after the last frost. Water evenly through the season for even ripening of the tapered pods. Harvest the fruit when deep red and dry it for grinding into a mild, aromatic powder.

planting.note: An Indian chile valued for deep red color and mild heat.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant into full sun once frost is past. Keep evenly watered so the pods develop their rich color. Harvest the fruit when deep red and dry it for the vivid, gently spicy powder used in Indian dishes.

planting.note: A wrinkled South Indian chile prized for color over heat.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant into warm soil in full sun. Grow through a long season with steady watering for full pod length. Harvest the long, wrinkled fruit when deep red and dry it for its rich color and mild flavor.

planting.note: A Chinese chile whose pods point upward toward the sky.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant into full sun after the last frost. The plants hold their pods upright, making them easy to spot. Harvest the small conical fruit when red and dry it for use in Sichuan cooking.

planting.note: A small round Pakistani chile with a strong heat.

directions: Sow seed indoors with warmth and transplant into full sun after frost. These heat-loving plants prefer well-drained soil and moderate watering. Harvest the small round pods when deep red and dry them for grinding into a hot chile powder.

planting.note: A tiny ornamental-edible chile with colorful upright pods.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant the dwarf plants into pots or the front of a bed. They cover themselves in small pods that shift from cream to orange to red. Harvest the little fruit at any color for a bright, sharp heat.

planting.note: A curiosity heirloom known for its oddly shaped pods.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant into full sun once the soil warms. Grow through the season with steady watering for good fruit set. Harvest the wrinkled, distinctively shaped pods when red for a moderate, jalapeno-like heat.

planting.note: A hotter New Mexico long green chile for roasting.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant into warm soil in full sun. Water steadily and feed to size up the long, tapering pods. Harvest green for roasting, or let the fruit ripen red for a sweeter yet still-hot chile.

planting.note: A golden Caribbean bonnet essential to island cooking.

directions: Sow seed early indoors with warmth and grow on for a long season. Transplant into a warm, sunny bed with rich soil and steady feeding. Harvest the bonnet-shaped pods when bright yellow for their fruity aroma and strong heat.

planting.note: Begin indoors two months ahead and move outdoors after the soil has warmed.

directions: Raise seedlings under warmth and harden them off before setting into fertile ground a foot and a half apart. Mulch to hold moisture and feed lightly through the season. Let the blocky fruit turn from green to a deep gold before cutting for the sweetest flavor.

common_name: King of the North Bell Pepper

planting.note: An early, cool-climate favorite; start indoors and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Sow indoors and grow sturdy transplants for setting out in short-season gardens. This open-pollinated variety sets and ripens fruit reliably even where summers run cool. Harvest the medium blocky peppers green or wait for them to turn full red.

planting.note: Begin indoors eight weeks early and transplant into warm soil after frost.

directions: Start seed warm and grow on before planting into rich ground with steady moisture. The blocky peppers shift from green to a rich chocolate-brown as they ripen. Wait for the full brown color for the deepest sweetness before cutting.

planting.note: Sow indoors in early spring and transplant once the ground is thoroughly warm.

directions: Grow seedlings under warmth and set the plants out with even spacing in full sun. Provide consistent water so the large thick-walled fruit sizes up without cracking. Let peppers ripen fully to bright orange for a very sweet, juicy flavor.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow stocky plants before setting into fertile, well-drained soil. The vigorous vines produce large blocky, deep-green fruit with smooth walls. Harvest firm and green, or leave to redden for a sweeter taste.

planting.note: Begin indoors about two months early and transplant into warm soil.

directions: Raise seedlings warm and set the leafy plants out where the canopy will shade the fruit. This mosaic-tolerant heirloom holds up well in warm regions with good moisture. Cut the large four-lobed peppers green, or ripen them red on the plant.

planting.note: An early ripener good for cool areas; start indoors and set out after frost.

directions: Grow transplants under warmth and set them into fertile ground after frost. This early variety colors up dependably in short-season gardens, turning brown outside and red within. Harvest once fruit reaches its rich chocolate hue for the fullest sweetness.

planting.note: A very early hybrid ideal for northern gardens; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Start seed indoors and grow on before transplanting into warm, well-drained soil. This hybrid sets fruit readily even in cool weather, rarely dropping blossoms. Harvest the medium three-lobed peppers green or let them ripen quickly to red.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant into warm soil once frost has passed.

directions: Grow transplants under warmth and space them evenly in a sunny bed. The blocky fruit emerges lavender-purple and matures through orange and red streaks. Harvest early for the striking violet color or wait for a fully ripe sweeter red.

planting.note: A disease-tolerant hybrid; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Sow seed warm and raise sturdy plants before setting into fertile soil. This vigorous hybrid resists bacterial leaf spot and yields large blocky green-to-red fruit. Cut peppers firm and green, or leave them to color fully red.

planting.note: A short-season heirloom; start indoors and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors and set stocky transplants into warm ground once frost is past. Bred for northern summers, it ripens medium blocky fruit reliably before fall. Harvest green or wait for the peppers to sweeten to full red.

planting.note: An Italian bull's horn type; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Raise seedlings warm and stake the tall plants that carry long curved fruit. Keep evenly watered so the horn-shaped peppers reach full length without stress. Let them ripen to deep red for the rich sweetness prized for roasting and frying.

planting.note: The yellow bull's horn form; start indoors and set out after frost.

directions: Sow seed warm and support the productive plants as the long fruit develops. Water steadily and feed lightly to fill out the tapering horns. Harvest once peppers turn glowing yellow for a mellow, sweet frying pepper.

planting.note: An award-winning bull's horn hybrid; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Grow transplants under warmth and set them into rich soil after frost. This reliable hybrid ripens early even in cooler summers, producing tapered six-inch fruit. Pick at glossy red for a sweet flavor excellent roasted or fried.

planting.note: A long Italian sweet pepper; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Start seed warm and stake the tall plants that bear long tapering fruit. Provide steady water and a rich bed so the peppers grow to full size. Ripen to deep red for the classic sweet, thin-walled Italian frying pepper.

planting.note: The golden form of Marconi; start indoors and set out after frost.

directions: Raise seedlings under warmth and support the plants as long fruit forms. Keep the soil evenly moist and feed lightly to size the tapering peppers. Harvest at rich golden-yellow for a sweet, tender pepper ideal for grilling.

planting.note: A yellow bull's horn hybrid; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow compact, well-branched transplants for setting out. This early hybrid ripens tapered fruit ahead of many long peppers. Harvest at bright yellow for a sweet, thin-walled frying pepper with heavy yields.

planting.note: A large Argentine frying pepper; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Start seed warm and stake the tall plants that carry very long tapered fruit. Water consistently so the big peppers, sometimes a foot long, size up well. Pick pale green for frying or let ripen to red for a very sweet flavor.

planting.note: The classic heart-shaped pimento; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Grow transplants under warmth and set them into fertile, well-drained soil. The thick-walled heart-shaped fruit is sweetest when fully ripe. Wait for a deep red color before harvesting for roasting, stuffing, or pimento cheese.

planting.note: An early pimento type for cool areas; start indoors and set out after frost.

directions: Start seed warm and set stocky plants out after frost in a sunny spot. This early variety ripens tapered pimento fruit dependably in short seasons. Harvest at glossy red for a sweet, thick-walled pepper good fresh or roasted.

planting.note: A flattened cheese-type pimento; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Raise seedlings warm and plant into rich soil with steady moisture. The squat, lobed tomato-shaped fruit has thick sweet walls when ripe. Harvest at full red for stuffing, roasting, or slicing fresh.

planting.note: A classic light-green frying pepper; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow on before setting productive plants into fertile soil. The thin-walled tapered fruit is usually picked while pale yellow-green for frying. Harvest young and light-colored, or let some ripen red for a sweeter flavor.

planting.note: A beloved Italian heirloom frying pepper; start indoors and set out after frost.

directions: Start seed warm and grow sturdy plants for setting into a sunny, fertile bed. The long thin curved fruit ripens abundantly in clusters through summer. Wait for the deep red color, when this pepper turns intensely sweet for frying.

planting.note: A Japanese frying pepper; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Grow transplants under warmth and set them into a sunny bed after frost. The small wrinkled fruit is picked green and immature for blistering and grilling. Harvest often at two to three inches; most are mild, though an occasional pepper runs hot.

planting.note: A Spanish tapas pepper; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Sow seed warm and set plants into a sunny, fertile spot after frost. Pick the small fruit young and green when they are mostly mild and sweet. Harvest frequently, since peppers left to grow larger turn noticeably hotter.

planting.note: A mild elongated pepper; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Start seed warm and grow compact plants for setting into fertile soil. The long tapered fruit ripens from pale yellow through orange to red. Pick yellow for a mild crisp pepper, or let ripen red for extra sweetness.

planting.note: A sweet waxy-yellow frying pepper; start indoors and set out after frost.

directions: Raise seedlings warm and plant them into a sunny bed after frost. The tapered waxy fruit is sweet and mild, unlike its hot-wax cousins. Harvest pale yellow for frying, or let some redden on the plant for sweeter flavor.

planting.note: A Cubanelle-type hybrid; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow productive transplants for a sunny, fertile bed. This early hybrid yields long light-green frying fruit ahead of many heirlooms. Pick at the pale yellow-green stage, or let peppers ripen to red for sweeter flavor.

planting.note: An Italian bull's horn frying pepper; start indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Start seed warm and set well-branched plants into fertile soil after frost. The long tapered fruit ripens early from green to a rich red. Harvest at full red for a sweet, thin-walled pepper excellent grilled or fried.

planting.note: Set transplants out only after the soil has warmed well past the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors about a quarter inch deep six to eight weeks before your last frost and keep the flats warm until sprouts appear. Move sturdy seedlings into fertile, well-drained beds once nights stay reliably mild, and mulch to hold steady moisture. Cut the large teardrop fruits while the skin is glossy and springs back to a light press, before it dulls and the seeds harden.

planting.note: Transplant into warm soil a couple of weeks after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow shallowly indoors in warm flats and pot up the vigorous seedlings before setting them out into rich, sun-baked ground. This tall Taiwanese heirloom carries slim lavender-purple fruits, so stake the branchy plants to keep the load off the soil. Harvest the long, tender fruits young when they snap cleanly, and pick often to keep new ones coming.

planting.note: Set plants out into warm ground after frost danger ends.

directions: Start seed indoors in warm flats and grow the seedlings on until they are stocky before setting them out. This early Asian hybrid sets long, thin-skinned fruits even in cooler or short-season gardens, making it a dependable early choice. Pick the glossy fruits young and often to encourage a long, heavy run of harvests.

planting.note: Transplant into warm, well-drained soil once frost has passed.

directions: Sow indoors and grow the seedlings warm before hardening them off for the garden. This early Japanese-type hybrid produces long, slim, deep-purple fruit on upright plants and yields well through the heat of summer. Harvest while the skin is taut and shining, cutting rather than pulling to avoid tearing the branches.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant the seedlings into full sun and rich soil after frost. Slender finger-length fruits form in clusters, so pick them small and tender for the best flavor. Frequent harvesting keeps the compact plants setting new fruit right through the season.

planting.note: Transplant well after frost into warm garden soil.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and set them into fertile, sunny beds once nights are warm. This Asian hybrid bears long, slim, pale pink-lavender fruits with mild, tender flesh and few seeds. Harvest the glossy fruits young and keep picking to sustain steady production.

planting.note: Plant out after frost when the soil has thoroughly warmed.

directions: Start seed in warm flats indoors and grow on before transplanting into full sun. This vigorous Asian-type hybrid produces long, deep-purple fruit prolifically over a long season. Cut fruits while glossy and firm, harvesting often to keep the plants bearing.

planting.note: Transplant into hot, sunny beds after all frost has passed.

directions: Sow indoors early and grow the heat-loving seedlings warm until they can go out into full sun. This Southeast Asian type sets long, slender, light-green fruits that stay tender and mild. Harvest young and often, cutting the fruit free to protect the branching stems.

planting.note: Set out transplants once the soil is warm and frost is past.

directions: Start this Italian heirloom indoors in warm flats and transplant into rich, sunny ground after the weather settles. It bears plump, rounded fruits shaded lavender-pink and white, prized for creamy, mild, non-bitter flesh. Pick when the skin is glossy and full, before the color fades, for the best texture.

planting.note: Transplant into warm beds a couple of weeks after the last frost.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors under warmth and set them out into fertile soil once summer heat arrives. This Florentine heirloom produces large, ribbed fruits in soft lavender, sometimes streaked white, with sweet, tender flesh. Harvest while the skin still shines and gives slightly to a gentle press.

planting.note: Transplant after frost once the soil has warmed thoroughly.

directions: Grow seedlings indoors in warm conditions and move them to rich, sunny beds after frost. This Italian heirloom sets large, round to slightly flattened, deep-purple fruits with dense, mild flesh well suited to grilling. Pick while the skin is taut and glossy for tender, low-seed slices.

planting.note: Set transplants out after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Start seed indoors and grow the seedlings on before transplanting into fertile, sunny ground. This reliable Italian-type hybrid produces glossy, deep-purple-black, teardrop fruits and sets well even where summers run cooler. Harvest firm, shining fruits by cutting the thick stem cleanly with pruners.

planting.note: Transplant into warm beds once frost danger has passed.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors under warmth and set them out into full sun after frost. This Italian-type hybrid yields plump, glossy, deep-purple fruits with a classic teardrop shape and firm, mild flesh. Cut fruits while the skin is bright and taut, harvesting regularly to keep new fruit forming.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into rich, warm garden soil.

directions: Start this Italian-type hybrid indoors and transplant the sturdy seedlings into full sun once the soil warms. It produces large, glossy, rounded violet fruits with tender, mild flesh and holds well on the plant. Harvest while the skin is shining and firm for the best eating quality.

planting.note: Transplant early after frost, as this variety fruits quickly.

directions: Sow indoors in warmth and grow on before moving seedlings to full sun after frost. This early Italian-type hybrid sets rounded, glossy violet fruits ahead of most, making it good for shorter seasons. Pick young while the skin gleams and the flesh stays tender and sweet.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; compact plants also grow well in containers.

directions: Start seed indoors and set the compact seedlings into full sun or a large pot after frost. This award-winning miniature hybrid bears small, elongated fruits streaked purple and white in clusters on tidy plants. Harvest the little fruits young and often; they stay sweet and seedless even if left a bit longer.

planting.note: Move plants out after frost; well suited to containers and small beds.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and transplant the compact plants into full sun or roomy containers once frost passes. This award-winning hybrid produces clusters of slender, deep-purple finger fruits with tender, non-bitter flesh. Pick them small at three to four inches, or let a few size up while staying seedless and mild.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; compact enough for pots and patios.

directions: Start indoors and set the tidy seedlings into full sun or large containers after frost. This award-winning white companion to Hansel bears clusters of slim, ivory finger fruits with sweet, tender flesh. Harvest them young and often to keep the plants producing heavily.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; bred for containers and small spaces.

directions: Sow indoors and set the dwarf, spineless seedlings into a pot or bed in full sun after frost. This award-winning miniature hybrid produces heavy flushes of small, deep-purple egg-shaped fruits very early. Pick the two-to-three-inch fruits often while glossy to keep the compact plant covered in more.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; compact habit suits patio pots.

directions: Start seed indoors and set the compact plants into containers or beds in full sun after frost. This baby Italian-type hybrid bears clusters of small, round, glossy purple fruits ideal for a single serving. Harvest the little fruits young and frequently to keep production rolling.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; dwarf plants thrive in containers.

directions: Grow seedlings indoors in warmth and set the dwarf plants into pots or beds in full sun after frost. This award-winning miniature hybrid carries small oval fruits striped purple and white on compact, tidy plants. Pick the fruits young while the stripes are crisp for tender, sweet flesh.

planting.note: Transplant into hot, sunny beds after frost has passed.

directions: Start seed indoors and give the seedlings warmth before moving them to full sun after frost. This Thai-type hybrid produces small, round, golf-ball fruits mottled green and white, used whole in curries. Harvest the firm little fruits while the green is bright, before they yellow and the seeds toughen.

planting.note: Plant out into hot ground well after the last frost.

directions: Sow indoors early and grow the heat-loving seedlings warm before transplanting into full sun. This Southeast Asian type sets small, round fruits marbled green and white that are picked firm and used whole. Harvest while the fruits are crisp and the coloring is vivid, before the seeds harden.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; tolerant of cooler summers.

directions: Start seed indoors and set the seedlings into full sun after frost. This early heirloom bears rounded, pale apple-green fruits with thin skin and mild, tender flesh, and it sets well in shorter seasons. Pick the fruits while glossy and light green, before they blush yellow and the seeds mature.

planting.note: Transplant into warm, sunny beds after frost.

directions: Grow seedlings indoors under warmth and transplant into full sun after frost. This white-fruited variety produces smooth, cylindrical ivory fruits with mild, mushroom-tinged flesh. Harvest while the skin is bright white and glossy, before it dulls to cream and the seeds set.

planting.note: Transplant into hot, sunny beds once frost has passed.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors in warmth and set them into full sun after frost. This Puerto Rican heirloom produces large, teardrop fruits in bright lavender-pink with sweet, mild, non-bitter flesh. Harvest while the skin is glossy and the color is vivid for the most tender eating.

planting.note: Plant out into hot ground after frost, as this type craves heat.

directions: Start seed indoors early and grow the seedlings warm before transplanting into full sun. This Iraqi heirloom produces very large, rounded, deep-purple-black fruits with dense, mild flesh well suited to roasting. Harvest while the skin is taut and glossy, before it dulls and the flesh coarsens.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; tall bushes tolerate heat and humidity.

directions: Grow seedlings indoors in warmth and set them into rich, sunny soil after frost. This heirloom forms tall, sturdy bushes that hold their large, oval purple fruits well off the ground and shrug off heat and disease. Harvest firm, glossy fruits by cutting the thick stems, picking regularly to prolong the season.

planting.note: Transplant into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors and set the seedlings into fertile, sunny beds after frost. This dependable Italian heirloom bears long, cylindrical, deep-violet fruits with tender, mild flesh over a long season. Harvest the glossy fruits young and cut them free to protect the branching stems.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Start seed indoors under warmth and move the seedlings to full sun after frost. This white-fruited Italian-type hybrid produces smooth, oval ivory fruits with mild, tender, non-bitter flesh. Harvest while the skin is bright white and glossy for the best texture.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; compact plants suit containers well.

directions: Start seed indoors and set the compact plants into pots or beds in full sun after frost. This dwarf variety bears clusters of slender, small purple fruits on tidy, ornamental plants with violet-tinged foliage. Pick the little fruits young and often to keep them tender and the plants productive.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; very compact and ideal for pots.

directions: Sow indoors and set the dwarf seedlings into small containers or the front of a bed in full sun after frost. This miniature variety covers itself in tiny, round, deep-purple fruits barely an inch across. Harvest the little fruits whole and often to keep the compact plant flowering and setting more.

planting.note: Plant out into hot ground well after frost for this heat-lover.

directions: Start seed indoors early and grow the seedlings warm before transplanting into full sun. This Indian heirloom bears rounded fruits blushed lavender and gold with green striping, ripening toward orange if left on the plant. Harvest while the fruits are still firm and lightly colored for the mildest, most tender flesh.

planting.note: Transplant after frost danger passes and soil has warmed.

directions: Sow indoors a quarter inch deep and grow warm until transplant size. Space plants generously and support the wide, branching habit with a cage. Pick fruit once the husk turns papery and the skin flushes violet for the sweetest flavor.

planting.note: Move transplants outdoors once the soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Begin seed indoors six weeks ahead of transplanting and keep the flat above 70 F for even sprouting. This vigorous strain yields large green fruit, so give each plant a strong cage. Gather tomatillos when the husks split and the fruit inside is firm and full.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost, barely covering it. The low, spreading plants drop their ripe husked fruit to the ground when ready. Collect the fallen papery husks and enjoy the golden berries fresh or in preserves.

planting.note: Set plants out once soil warms and frost risk ends.

directions: Sow indoors in warm soil and transplant the sprawling seedlings after frost. Mulch beneath the plants so dropped fruit stays clean and easy to find. Sweep up the husk-wrapped golden berries once they fall and the husks turn tan.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings or plants once nights stay above 50 F.

directions: Most gardeners start pepino from cuttings, which root readily in moist soil. Grow in a warm, sheltered spot and stake the brittle stems to keep fruit off the ground. Harvest when the pale fruit develops purple streaks and yields slightly to a gentle squeeze.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into warm garden soil.

directions: Sow indoors a quarter inch deep and set out sturdy plants after frost. The berries are only edible fully ripe and cooked, so never eat them green. Harvest clusters when the fruit turns dull purple-black and slightly soft, then cook into pies or preserves.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Start seed indoors early since sprouting is slow, and handle seedlings carefully. The whole plant is covered in sharp spines, so wear gloves and give it space. Harvest the bright red cherry-like fruit when the spiny husk splits open and the fruit softens.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Start seed indoors six weeks ahead and set out deep transplants after frost. This vigorous indeterminate cherry needs tall support and produces long trusses all summer. Pick the golden-orange fruit as soon as it colors, since ripe cherries can split if left on the vine.

planting.note: Transplant after frost when nights stay above 55 F.

directions: Start seed indoors eight weeks early and set out sturdy plants into warm soil. Keep moisture steady to help the plants hold their heavy fruit. Harvest the large dark-green heart-shaped pods for fresh chiles, or let them ripen red and dry as ancho.

planting.note: Transplant into warm soil after frost danger passes.

directions: Sow indoors in warm mix and grow to a good size before transplanting. The compact, bushy plants make fine container subjects and hold hundreds of small pods upright. Harvest the tiny peppers green or red, both intensely hot.

planting.note: Set out transplants once nights are reliably warm.

directions: Start seed indoors eight weeks ahead and transplant after frost into warm soil. Keep the plants evenly watered for a steady flush of long, tapering pods. Harvest the fruit pale yellow for mild flavor, or leave it to ripen through orange to red.

planting.note: Set out transplants after frost into warm, rich soil.

directions: Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks early and keep the flat warm for good germination. Transplant into warm soil and cage or stake the productive plants. Harvest the slender lavender-purple fruit young and tender while the skin stays glossy.

planting.note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed and the soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seeds about an inch deep directly in warm garden soil and keep evenly moist until sprouts emerge. Space plants closely so the bushes support one another and avoid working among wet foliage to reduce disease. Pick the round, meaty pods every few days while they snap cleanly and before the seeds swell.

planting.note: One of the first beans that can go in as soil warms in spring.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in cool-to-warm soil, where this reliable variety germinates better than most. Water steadily once flowering begins to keep the round green pods tender. Harvest young pods regularly to encourage a long, productive run.
